summary: We talked through the tension Jordan keeps circling — valuing empathy and connection while being trained to think like an "expert." He opened up about the presentation nerves he couldn't name, and we traced them back to caring a lot about how the group received him. Ended by setting two goals to carry into Unit 2.

transcript.speaker: teacher
transcript.text: I wanted to start with something I noticed across almost everything you wrote. You describe yourself as valuing empathy and human connection over technical mastery — but you also list yourself under the "expert" and "macho" mythos. Where do you feel that pull?

transcript.speaker: student
transcript.text: Honestly, all the time. Like, I want to be the person who reads the room and lifts other people up. But then in my aerospace classes, being the one who has the answer is kind of the whole currency. So I catch myself wanting to be the expert even when I say I don't care about that.

transcript.speaker: teacher
transcript.text: That's really honest. Does it ever go the other way — where wanting to connect gets in the way of just owning something?

transcript.speaker: student
transcript.text: Yeah. In group work I'll defer even when I actually know I'm right, because I don't want to be the macho engineer guy. So I kind of erase myself. Which isn't leadership either.

transcript.speaker: teacher
transcript.text: Let's go to something you flagged yourself in the self-assessment. You gave yourself A's across the board, but you also said you were surprised how nervous you felt presenting — and that you couldn't figure out why. What do you make of that gap now?

transcript.speaker: student
transcript.text: I've done tons of public speaking, so it threw me. But I think... it's the same thing. I cared so much whether the group thought I was one of them. It wasn't the speaking. It was, are these people going to accept me. That's what made my hands shake.

transcript.speaker: teacher
transcript.text: That's a genuinely useful thing to know about yourself. It connects straight to your followership interview — you came in thinking teams rise and fall on the leader, and left thinking followership is the engine. Did that shift feel uncomfortable?

transcript.speaker: student
transcript.text: At first, yeah. I'd built a whole identity around being a leader. Realizing the follower role is where teams actually work kind of dented my ego. But it also felt like relief? Like I don't have to carry the whole thing.

transcript.speaker: teacher
transcript.text: So here's what I'm hearing, and tell me if I'm wrong. A lot of your growth edge isn't a skill — it's letting yourself take up space without needing to be liked for it. Does that land?

transcript.speaker: student
transcript.text: Yeah. That's it exactly. I hadn't put it that way but that's the thread.

transcript.speaker: teacher
transcript.text: Okay. Let's turn that into something we actually work on next unit. What would you want to try?

transcript.speaker: student
transcript.text: I want to practice stating what I think in a group without softening it into a question. And I want to actually change a study habit instead of just writing that I should — because I keep naming adaptability as my weak spot and then not doing anything about it.

goals.text: In group work, state my actual position once before deferring — practice taking up space without needing to be liked for it.
goals.connectsTo: The "expert vs. macho" pull and the presentation nerves — both trace back to caring how the group receives me.

goals.text: Pick one concrete study habit and change it this unit (not just name it), then reflect on whether it held.
goals.connectsTo: The recurring "adaptability is my growth edge" theme that hasn't yet become action.
